lesson: Unit 02 Studio — Capstone Build (3D solid, volume & surface area)
banner: Week 22 · Unit 02 Capstone — Build & Verify a Solid
essential_question: Can you build a solid, then prove its volume and surface area with your own measurements?
objective: Design and build (or 3D-print) a rectangular solid, cylinder, prism, pyramid, or cone; measure its dimensions; and verify computed volume and surface area against real measurement.

agenda.title: Brief — Today You Build & Prove a Solid

content.heading: Unit 02 skills, one build
bullets: Volume formulas (L14, L15) — V = Bh for prisms/cylinders, the ⅓ formulas for pyramids/cones.
Surface area & nets (L16) — unfold the solid on paper and add up every face.
If you choose a round solid, bring back circle/π facts from L12–L13.
The test: build or print a real solid, then verify BOTH its volume and its surface area by measurement.

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Choose a rectangular solid, cylinder, cone, pyramid, or prism. Decide real-world dimensions. If it helps, sketch a net before you build or send a file to the printer.

content.heading: Make it real
bullets: Cut, fold, stack, or 3D-print the solid.
Label every measurable dimension: radius/height, or length/width/height.
Keep faces and edges clean enough to measure with a ruler.

content.preview: Session 3: final mixed review, then MUS Unit Test II on your own.

success_criteria.text: I can build a real (or 3D-printed) solid and label its measurable dimensions
I can measure a solid's dimensions and use them to compute its volume
I can measure/compute a solid's surface area and explain any gap between computed and real values

questions.prompt: Measure the dimensions of your solid (radius/height, or length/width/height) and record them with units.
questions.answer: Any consistent measured dimensions with units.
questions.explain: Every volume/surface-area formula depends on getting real, correctly-labeled measurements first.

questions.prompt: Using your measured dimensions, compute the volume of your solid. Show the formula you used.
questions.answer: e.g. V = Bh, V = πr²h, or V = (1/3)Bh depending on the solid — must show the substituted formula and cubic units.
questions.explain: Different solids use different volume formulas; the shape you chose decides which one applies.

questions.prompt: Compute the surface area of your solid using your measured dimensions (or your net), showing the formula.
questions.answer: Appropriate surface-area formula for the chosen solid, substituted with measured values, in square units.
questions.explain: Surface area is the total area of every face — a net makes this easy to see and add up.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which was trickier to build honestly — getting the volume right or the surface area right — and what tripped you up?
